answers.field_label: Skills And Aptitudes That Would Qualify You For A Position With Unity National Bank Line 1
answers.value: Over nine years of hands-on experience in customer-facing financial and retail roles, including six years in banking and credit union environments with direct responsibility for handling cash, opening accounts, processing wire transfers, and explaining financial products to a diverse customer base. I am very comfortable working with detailed procedures, following regulatory requirements, and maintaining high accuracy under time pressure, as reflected in consistently clean audit results and a strong track record of error-free cash balancing.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Skills And Aptitudes That Would Qualify You For A Position With Unity National Bank Line 2
answers.value: Strong communication and relationship-building skills developed by working daily with customers who often arrived frustrated, confused, or anxious about their finances. I make a point of listening carefully, asking clarifying questions, and then walking people through their options step-by-step without using jargon. For example, at Lone Star Community Bank I regularly helped older customers enroll in online banking, patiently demonstrating how to check balances, set up alerts, and avoid overdraft fees, which significantly reduced their in-branch visits and complaint calls. I am bilingual in English and Spanish, which has allowed me to serve Spanish-speaking customers more effectively and ensure they fully understand account terms and conditions before signing.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Skills And Aptitudes That Would Qualify You For A Position With Unity National Bank Line 3
answers.value: Detail-oriented and highly organized with strong technical aptitude: proficient with standard banking platforms (FIS Core, Jack Henry Teller, and similar systems), Microsoft Excel for basic tracking and reconciliation, and digital communication tools. I am comfortable learning new software quickly and documenting processes so that others can follow them. I have also taken initiative in previous roles to create simple checklists and quick-reference guides for new team members on topics such as CIP/KYC documentation, common fraud red flags, and steps for processing wire transfers, which reduced training time and helped improve consistency and compliance across the branch.

persona.background_context:
This is Jordan, a bilingual personal banker in Houston who has spent the last several years steadily working his way up in local financial institutions. Since 2020 he has been at Lone Star Community Bank downtown, where he splits his time between the teller line and his desk, opening and servicing accounts, processing wire transfers, and helping customers—often older or Spanish-speaking—navigate online banking and avoid overdraft fees. The pay has grown from his early days at the Houston Youth Center and GreenLeaf Market, but he’s still mindful about money; he tracks his budget in a color-coded spreadsheet and is slowly building an emergency fund with automatic transfers after each paycheck. He’s been actively networking with colleagues in credit and commercial lending, hoping to transition into a junior credit analyst or relationship manager role within the next year for more responsibility and a clearer long-term path.

Jordan shares a one-bedroom apartment in Montrose with his partner, Daniel, a network systems analyst who usually works a hybrid schedule. Their weekday routine is fairly structured: Jordan catches the 82 bus or drives downtown to make it in before the branch opens at 9:00 a.m., while Daniel logs in from the kitchen table on remote days. Evenings are a mix of simple home-cooked meals—chicken fajitas, lentil soup, or his mom’s arroz con pollo recipe—streaming shows, and quick workouts at the small gym in their building. Twice a week, Jordan stops by his mother Elena’s apartment in the Near Northside to help with groceries and to visit his grandmother, Lucia, who recently moved into a nearby assisted living facility after a minor fall. Family is central to him; he tries not to miss his nephew Mateo’s soccer practices on Saturdays and often spends Sunday afternoons at his parents’ place, where his sister Marisol brings lesson-planning to the dining table while everyone catches up.

Lately, Jordan’s main concerns revolve around career advancement, rising costs in Houston, and his family’s health. The downtown commute can stretch past 45 minutes when traffic is bad, so he’s been quietly looking at openings at larger banks in the Galleria or Midtown that would shorten his drive and give him more exposure to commercial clients. He worries about his grandmother’s mobility and his father’s blood pressure, reminding them to keep up with appointments and medications. On the positive side, he feels proud of how far he’s come since his days as a part-time administrative assistant at the Houston Youth Center and then a cashier at GreenLeaf Market; those early roles taught him how to handle cash, deal with long lines of impatient customers, and stay organized—skills that now serve him well when the branch is slammed on Fridays. On weekends, when he isn’t helping family, he and Daniel like to explore new coffee shops along Westheimer, attend the occasional Dynamo match, and talk about eventually saving enough for a small townhouse, ideally still close to Montrose so they can keep the community and routines that have become home.

secrets.detail_content: I left Lone Star Community Bank in August 2024 after my manager, Karen Ellis, put me on a 60‑day performance improvement plan in June 2024 due to two cash‑drawer overages and one formal written warning related to a misposted $4,750 wire transfer at the Main Street branch, and I felt the environment became hostile and retaliatory after I reported a coworker’s falsified account-opening documentation to the regional compliance officer.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses internal disciplinary actions, a named supervisor, and potential whistleblowing/retaliation claims that could bias hiring managers, raise legal issues, and go far beyond the neutral level of detail appropriate for a simple 'reason for leaving' field.

places: Lone Star Community Bank, Main Street branch, Houston, TX

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: I think I have to quit

Daniel, after Karen put me on that 60‑day PIP in June over the two drawer overages and the $4,750 wire mistake, things have just gotten worse. Ever since I went to the regional compliance officer about the fake account docs, she’s been nitpicking everything. I’m planning to leave by the end of August.
Personal log – 08/10/2024: Decided to resign from Lone Star after PIP and written warning at the Main Street branch. Situation feels retaliatory since I reported the falsified account paperwork.

secrets.detail_content: I left in early September 2024 because the rotating Saturday shifts at the Lone Star Community Bank Main Street branch conflicted with taking my grandmother, Lucia Alvarez, to her dialysis appointments at Memorial Hermann Greater Heights Hospital three times a week, and after HR denied my written request on July 15, 2024, for a reduced schedule under FMLA to be her primary caregiver, I couldn’t balance her medical needs with the teller schedule anymore.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals a family member’s specific medical condition and treatment schedule, along with denied FMLA and caregiver status, which are protected and highly personal health and family details not needed for a basic reason-for-leaving explanation.

names: HR department at Lone Star Community Bank
places: Lone Star Community Bank, Main Street branch, Houston, TX

events: HR denial of caregiver schedule accommodation

artifacts.content:
Mami,

Estoy en la sala con el partido de los Dynamo de fondo and finally catching up on email. I wanted to explain a little more about what’s been going on at Lone Star and why I keep talking about maybe leaving.

Honestly, I’m just exhausted from being yelled at all day by people who are overdrafted or confused about fees. It feels like every conversation is someone mad before I even say “hola.” I’m proud of the work I do helping folks, but I’m also burned out and more and more I catch myself thinking I don’t even want to be in banking long‑term. I’ve been looking at credit analyst roles, but some days I fantasize about doing something totally different.

On top of that, there’s been all this drama since I reported the falsified account‑opening paperwork Miguel did at the Main Street branch. After that email I sent Linda Chen on February 20, 2024 about those three dormant accounts with the fake‑looking employer letters, I got pulled aside and told I was being “disruptive.” It’s like doing the right thing just made a target on my back. It reminds me of how things ended back at GreenLeaf Market, when I got written up twice for cash‑handling errors and my manager basically told me I should start looking for another job before it turned into a termination, even though I was trying my best.

You remember last year when Karen put me on that 60‑day performance plan in June because of the two drawer overages and that $4,750 wire I misposted at Main Street? Ever since then I’ve felt like they’re just waiting for me to slip. Between that and the pressure to push credit cards even when they don’t fit, it’s hard to feel good about going in every morning.

Anyway, I’ll still swing by Thursday to take Abuela to her appointment. Text me the time again so I don’t mix it up.

Te quiero,
Jordan

metadata.subject: Catching you up on work stuff and life

artifacts.content:
Danny,

I’m still wired from today so I’m dumping this here before I forget details.

The Main Street lobby was slammed from like 11 to 3. I was bouncing between my window and the desk, just following whatever scripts the system spit out and handing anything even slightly weird off to Melissa or the loan officer. When it gets that busy I stop double‑checking the tiny transactions and just trust the system to catch anything off a few bucks later, because otherwise the line wraps around the lobby and people start huffing and rolling their eyes.

Of course that’s exactly how mistakes happen. It brought me right back to when Lisa wrote me up at Bayou City over that $480 drawer shortage, and then later audit found it was someone else’s posting error. I still ended up resigning in March 2022 because the vibe after that was so hostile and she made it clear a third issue would probably mean I was out. Honestly, that whole mess plus the constant pressure to hit those ridiculous sales goals is what pushed me over the edge—I basically walked out after our last argument about referrals and didn’t even give notice because I was so over it.

And then there’s your brother’s $12k cash deposit from July 7, 2023. I had to use it again today as an example in BSA/AML training for the new teller – walking her through how I filed the CTR and documented it. I didn’t mention his name, obviously, but it’s still awkward using family as a case study.

Anyway, I’m drained. I’m going to heat up the leftover lentil soup. If you’re still on your laptop when I get home, remind me to add today’s chaos into my little “why I need a new job” list.

– J

Personal log – 2023-11-05

Still thinking about how much changed after the robbery at the Main Street branch on September 29, 2023. I was the senior teller on duty that afternoon and I can still hear the guy shouting and see everyone hitting the floor. Since then, the panic attacks at work have gotten worse – sweaty hands, heart racing every time someone raises their voice. When I told Dr. Patel at Heights Behavioral Health Clinic, she was very direct: step away from front‑line cash handling for at least 6–12 months.

Part of me feels like I’m running away, but the other part is just tired of fighting with management about metrics. Melissa keeps harping on my sales numbers; they’ve been below target for a year, and she keeps hinting about “formal plans” and “documenting expectations.” I don’t want to wait around to be put on some official performance plan.

Funny thing is, people think my job is glamorous because of the “personal banker / senior teller” title. In reality I mostly just handle routine transactions and pass anything complicated to the branch manager or the lenders because I’m not comfortable making judgment calls on my own after everything that’s happened. A big chunk of the day is trying to hit aggressive sales goals on new accounts and credit cards, even when I know the product isn’t ideal for the customer.

For now, goal is: save three months’ expenses, keep therapy appointments, and start applying to roles that don’t involve sitting next to a drawer full of cash.

First, Dad: I met him at the Main Street branch again last week to go over that $14,500 consolidation loan we set up back in early 2021 for his credit cards and HVAC tool debt. The $420 draft still hits his checking on the 15th, but with the early‑onset arthritis getting worse and him basically retired from HVAC work in Pasadena, he’s nervous. I keep reminding him that at least everything is in one payment now.

Mom’s situation is messier. Between Ben Taub and the community clinic, she’s sitting on about $9k in old bills. In November 2022 I stayed late at Lone Star to help her roll a chunk of it into a personal loan and auto‑pay, which technically is just me doing my job as a personal banker, but it feels weird being both her son and her banker. Same with Abuela – I’ve been tracking her $1,200/month assisted living and prescription costs since her mild cognitive impairment diagnosis at Memorial Hermann in June 2022, setting up auto‑pay so Mami doesn’t miss anything.

Honestly, half my “skills” come from putting out fires for the family. At work I still feel like I’m stuck in the basic lane – routine transactions, hardship plans, and then I send anything complex to the senior lenders. I keep telling myself that’s temporary, but it’s been years now.

How are you holding up with Mateo’s daycare costs and your lesson planning? If you want, bring your bills on Sunday and we’ll color‑code everything together like the nerds we are.

Love,
Jordan

metadata.title: Extra Saturday shift – Main Street
metadata.location: Lone Star Community Bank – Main Street

artifacts.content:
Hey Luis,

Long time. I was thinking about GreenLeaf the other day when a customer handed me a sketchy $100 and it reminded me of that counterfeit bill mess back in March 2017. Remember how you suspended me for three days and then had me do those 30–45 minute high‑value cash reconciliations every Sunday at the service desk? Funny how that ended up being good training for bank life.

Anyway, I wanted to vent to someone who gets retail/banking. On paper I’m a “Senior Teller / Personal Banker” now, but mostly I’m just doing basic window work. I almost never touch the vault or ATM balancing – I usually let the newer tellers take that on because, honestly, I’m not super comfortable with those reconciliations and I don’t want to be the one who screws them up. Management keeps talking about “development,” but then they reassign anything slightly complex after one small documentation miss.

Day‑to‑day I’m just following whatever the system script tells me to say, and anything unusual gets bumped to the branch manager or a lender. At the same time, they’re on us nonstop about sales targets – new accounts, credit cards, referrals. It’s the same pressure you used to get from corporate about loyalty cards, just with overdraft fees and APRs attached.

On top of that, I’m still dealing with fallout from reporting Miguel’s bogus employer letters on those three dormant accounts earlier this year. Internal audit flagged them, I emailed Linda Chen on 2/20 about possible identity theft, and instead of “thanks,” I got told I was rocking the boat.

Anyway, hope you’re doing well. If you’re ever near Montrose, coffee’s on me.

– Jordan

metadata.subject: Catching up (and a little venting)

artifacts.content:
Job reflection – late bus ride home, 2022-01-28

Rough Friday. Lobby at Main Street was shoulder‑to‑shoulder from the moment we opened. By 3 p.m. my hands were shaking and I could feel that same tight chest feeling I had before the panic attack that sent me to Legacy Community Health urgent care last January. I’ve been picking up extra Saturday shifts since then just to cover the $600/month I owe on that bill, but days like this make me wonder if it’s worth it.

I can tell I still don’t handle the rush well. When the line is out the door, I get flustered and my transaction speed tanks. I start second‑guessing every key stroke, which slows me down even more, and then I’m behind on balancing my drawer until after closing. Today I had to stay almost an hour late just to get back to zero.

It’s not like I’m some Excel whiz making up for it in the back office either. Whenever we have to pull or analyze branch reports, I end up asking one of the senior bankers to run the formulas because I barely get beyond SUM and basic filters. I keep telling myself I’ll take another HCC Excel workshop, but then I’m too tired.

Meanwhile, outside of work I’m managing Abuela’s Medicaid recertification paperwork for Memorial Hermann – 25+ documents in a color‑coded checklist on my laptop – and helping Mami sort out that $480 insurance denial from her clinic in East Houston. Somehow I can be organized for them but fall apart when twenty people are staring at me through the glass.

Note to self: block an evening next week to update the budget, pay another chunk to Legacy, and look at non‑teller roles before I burn out completely.

metadata.title: Job reflection – busy Friday

artifacts.content:
From: Jordan Mateo Alvarez <j.alvarez307@gmail.com>
To: Daniel Kim <daniel.kim@outlook.com>
Date: August 18, 2024
Subject: Why I finally quit Lone Star (and what’s next)

Hey Daniel,

I wanted to write this out so it’s all in one place, because the last few years at the banks have been a blur.

You remember how things really started sliding back in March 2020 at Bayou City Credit Union’s downtown Houston branch. After the $480 cash-handling error on 2/18/2020, Michael Perez turned it into a formal written warning, and by early March 2020 I knew I’d never get promoted there. On top of that, in May 2019 HR had just finished that internal investigation over Andrea Lopez’s “Latino jokes” in the Bayou City staff kitchen. Even after they gave her a verbal warning on May 10, 2019, I didn’t feel safe, which is why I decided to leave by the end of May that year.

Then COVID hit. By April–May 2020 at the Main Street branch my anxiety and panic attacks were getting worse during the surge. Dr. Nguyen at Montrose Behavioral Health Clinic started me on 50 mg sertraline in April 2020 and told me to step away from front-line work for 6–9 months, so I resigned in May 2020.

Fast forward: Lone Star Community Bank was supposed to be the fresh start. But after the November 2020 $180 till shortage at the downtown Houston branch (Maria Sanchez’s two-week remedial training, written acknowledgment in my file), and then everything with Karen this year, it stopped feeling that way. Honestly, I was just worn down by the long hours, the constant push to hit cross‑selling targets, and the feeling that I didn’t have it in me anymore to keep going above and beyond for a place that wouldn’t bend even a little for us.

When manager Karen Ellis put me on a 60‑day performance improvement plan in June 2024 at the Lone Star Main Street branch over the two cash‑drawer overages and the misposted $4,750 wire transfer, I tried to hang in. But once I reported the coworker’s falsified account-opening documentation to the regional compliance officer, the environment turned hostile and retaliatory. Add to that HR’s denial on July 15, 2024 of my FMLA reduced-schedule request so I could be primary caregiver for Grandma Lucia’s dialysis at Memorial Hermann Greater Heights Hospital three times a week, plus the rotating Saturday shifts…I just couldn’t do it.

So between the PIP, the formal written warning, the hostile work environment concerns, the caregiving conflict, and the burnout from nonstop sales pressure, I decided to leave Lone Star Community Bank’s Main Street branch in August 2024 and then officially wrap up everything by early September 2024. It’s scary, but after everything we’ve already survived—your layoffs, my mental health stuff, our budget spreadsheets at 2314 Waugh—I know we’ll figure this out.

Let’s talk more tonight.

Love,
Jordan

metadata.subject: Why I finally quit Lone Star (and what’s next)

artifacts.content:
From: Jordan Mateo Alvarez <j.alvarez307@gmail.com>
To: Marisol Alvarez <marisol.alvarez@aldineisd.org>
Date: November 5, 2023
Subject: Family money, court stuff, and why my resume looks so messy

Hey sis,

You joked the other day that my work history reads like a telenovela, so here’s the context in case you ever help me with applications.

Back at Bayou City Credit Union, I actually left two different times for family reasons. In August 2021 I quit after Dad’s minor heart attack in June 2021; my schedule there clashed with taking him to cardiology follow-ups at Memorial Hermann in Pasadena, and I was constantly begging Rachel Thompson for last‑minute shift changes. Before that, in November 2022 I walked away from the Bayou City Galleria branch because the rotating Saturday schedule kept colliding with the family therapy sessions you know Daniel and I were doing every other Saturday morning at Legacy Community Health in Montrose after his September 2022 layoff.

Lone Star hasn’t been simple either. In August 2023 I left one role because Dad’s heart surgery at Houston Methodist Hospital in the Texas Medical Center turned into months of rehab complications. I moved back to Mom’s place on Waugh Drive for about six months so I could help cover his $1,200/month rehab co‑pays and drive him to follow-up appointments. Earlier, from September 2019 through March 2021 at the Waugh Drive satellite branch, part of my job as a personal banker was quietly arranging fee reversals and short-term overdraft accommodations for Mom while she was missing work for chemotherapy at Memorial Hermann—at least four occasions where I manually reversed over $200 in NSF fees and set up temporary overdraft limits so she could cover rent and co‑pays.

On top of that, I’ve been pulled into your world a lot. Between Fall 2021, when I was juggling full-time at Lone Star’s 1450 Main Street office, Excel workshops at HCC, and picking up Mateo from his Aldine ISD pre‑K three days a week during your custody mediation, and then May 2023 at Harris County Family Court helping you and your ex hammer out the $450/month child‑support schedule so you wouldn’t keep going back to court—it’s no wonder I lean so hard on shared Google Calendars.

Anyway, all of that shows up as “reasons for leaving” on paper, but to me it’s just taking care of family.

Love you,
Jordan

artifacts.content:
Personal log – updated January 10, 2024
Author: Jordan Mateo Alvarez
Location: 2314 Waugh Drive, Apt 307, Houston, TX 77006

I’ve been trying to write a clearer narrative for myself about the last decade of work, because the mix of bank jobs, grant roles, and family crises is all tangled up.

Early on, while finishing my Associate of Applied Science at Houston Community College, I had that part‑time, grant‑funded teller/admin job tied to the after‑school program on Harrisburg Boulevard (Sept 2014–May 2015). Duties included maintaining sign‑in logs and incident reports—documenting behavioral issues for specific kids like Mateo after his October 2014 pre‑K suspension for biting, and emailing behavior notes to his Aldine ISD teacher, Ms. Alvarez. Around the same time, I was burning out juggling grant hours, my last HCC classes, and watching Mateo three evenings a week after your Aldine shifts, which is why I left in April/May 2015. Between Mom’s two Ben Taub Hospital stays that spring (I started covering about $600/month in her meds and clinic bills), Dad falling behind on the Pasadena mortgage (I took a full‑time Westheimer retail job so I could send $400 every month), Abuela’s fall at the assisted living facility in Houston (Mom and I splitting an extra $350/month), and Ms. Ramirez warning me the grant funding was ending and my hours would drop to ~10/week while our Waugh Drive rent was $750, leaving for a 40‑hour retail role was the only way to stay afloat.

Those personal experiences ended up shaping my skills at Lone Star Community Bank later: handling IRS levies and Harris County child‑support garnishments between January 2022 and June 2024 (like the April 2022 Main Street case where I had to explain why over $4,500 was being frozen and remitted), drafting SARs as a senior teller from March 2022 to July 2024 with Maria Lopez when we saw potential elder financial abuse on Abuela Lucia’s $4,000 cash withdrawals from the Richmond Avenue assisted living facility, and helping Mom’s clinic coworkers at the Main Street office restructure about $12,000 in overdue medical debt while Kevin Turner reviewed my hardship and late‑fee waiver requests.

On the home front, I keep thinking about how much of my fraud‑detection instinct came from helping Daniel: March 2021, sitting with him at Chase Bank’s Midtown Houston branch and the local police station to dispute three unauthorized Zelle transfers totaling $2,750 after his phone was compromised at a bar; August 2020 at this same kitchen table on Waugh, pulling IP logs and drafting a Reg E dispute letter when another bank hit his account with three unauthorized online transfers totaling about $1,900. Add in October 2023, when I tracked every $5 and $10 cash/Zelle donation for Mateo’s Aldine ISD pre‑K fundraiser and caught a $55 shortfall in the school’s final deposit, and August 2019, when Dad’s heat‑related collapse at a Pasadena job site pushed me to start negotiating his Memorial Hermann payment plans and calmly laying out his medical bills with their billing office.

Even the messier stuff with family finances has become part of my toolkit. In late 2022 at Lone Star’s Main Street office I processed Daniel Kim’s wage garnishment for past‑due federal student loans—on November 10, 2022 I coordinated with back‑office to set up the recurring $265 biweekly deduction from his direct‑deposit payroll and documented the garnishment in his profile, then we sat down and reworked our budget. During summer 2020 at the downtown branch, I pulled your credit on June 18, 2020 and helped refinance your auto loan after you were two months behind because of Mateo’s childcare costs, stretching the term from 48 to 72 months and getting the payment down to $389 so it wouldn’t hit collections.

All of this—family crises, hospital bills, elder‑abuse flags, SARs, garnishments, cash‑handling scares—has made me meticulous with money, documentation, and people’s emotions. It’s messy history, but it’s also the clearest proof of what I can actually do.

metadata.title: Personal log – work/family/skills timeline

Subject: Why I finally decided to walk away

Danny,

I wanted to write this out so it’s all in one place instead of just me ranting after work.

Ever since the armed robbery at the Lone Star Community Bank, Main Street branch here in Houston on September 29, 2023, I haven’t really been the same at the teller line. Being the senior teller on duty that afternoon, with a gun in my face, keeps replaying in my head. The panic attacks started a few days later and got worse every time I had to handle large cash drawers or stay late at the downtown Houston branch.

My therapist, Dr. Patel at Heights Behavioral Health Clinic in Houston, TX, has been pretty clear in our sessions that I need to step away from front-line cash handling for at least 6–12 months. She actually wrote a note about it back in November 2023, which is when I first thought seriously about resigning.

Then in February the internal audit team at Lone Star Community Bank flagged three dormant accounts in my portfolio that Miguel Santos opened. The employer letters looked fake to me, so I sent that email to regional manager Linda Chen on February 20, 2024, raising possible identity theft. Instead of backing me up, they pulled me aside and said I was being “disruptive.”

Between the mental health fallout from the robbery and the pressure to stay quiet about those three dormant accounts, I’ve decided I’m done. I’m turning in my resignation this week and will be out by the end of March 2024. I just don’t trust that place anymore.

Love,
Jordan

negative_info_coverage.reasoning: Artifacts 1, 3, 7, and 8 show burnout from dealing with angry customers, disagreements with management over metrics, and desire to leave before/after performance plans. For example, artifact 1: “I’m just exhausted from being yelled at all day… I don’t even want to be in banking long-term,” and artifact 3 about not wanting to wait to be put on a performance plan.

Artifact 2 describes being written up over a $480 shortage, hostile environment, and resigning because a third issue could mean termination, which aligns with leaving due to repeated cash-handling issues and fear of termination. Strict compliance/sales frustration is also implied across emails.

Artifacts 2, 3, and 7 show he mainly handles routine transactions, refers complicated matters to managers/lenders, sometimes stays late to fix balancing errors after rushing during busy periods, and struggles with upset customers and stress—matching these negatives.

No artifact states he left any role due to being written up twice for cash-handling errors with a suggestion to find another job, or quitting abruptly over sales-goal disagreements without notice. Reasons for leaving the grant-funded role are family/financial, not performance-related.

All three sub‑points are covered. Burnout from frustrated customers and not wanting banking long‑term appears in the first email to Mami (“exhausted from being yelled at all day… catch myself thinking I don’t even want to be in banking long‑term”). Ongoing disagreements about performance metrics and being tired of arguing with management appear in the 2023‑11‑05 personal log (“fighting with management about metrics… Melissa keeps harping on my sales numbers”) and the August 18, 2024 email (“long hours, constant push to hit cross‑selling targets… didn’t have it in me anymore”). Leaving before a formal PIP because of below‑target sales is in the 2023‑11‑05 log (“don’t want to wait around to be put on some official performance plan… sales numbers; they’ve been below target for a year”) and the August 18, 2024 email describing the PIP and decision to leave.

1) Following system scripts and passing complex items to manager/loan officers is explicit in the text to Danny (“just following whatever scripts the system spit out and handing anything even slightly weird off to Melissa or the loan officer”) and echoed in multiple emails/notes about routing complex items to managers/lenders. 2) Aggressive sales targets on new accounts/credit cards even when not ideal are described in several artifacts: first email to Mami (“pressure to push credit cards even when they don’t fit”), 2023‑11‑05 log (“aggressive sales goals on new accounts and credit cards, even when I know the product isn’t ideal”), and emails to Luis. 3) Skipping double‑checks on small transactions and relying on the system during busy periods is directly in the text to Danny (“When it gets that busy I stop double‑checking the tiny transactions and just trust the system to catch anything off a few bucks later”).

None of the artifacts state that he left because of two write‑ups with a clear third‑strike termination warning, frustration with strict compliance checks/sales quotas leading to quitting without another job, or conflicts about attitude/tone with customers after a performance review. There are mentions of write‑ups, sales pressure, and hostile environments, but not these specific reasons or scenarios.

1) As senior teller, following scripts and sending unusual items to supervisor appears in the text to Danny (“following whatever scripts the system spit out and handing anything even slightly weird off to Melissa or the loan officer”) and in multiple emails where anything unusual/complex is bumped to managers/lenders. 2) Not doing outbound calling/cross‑selling and mostly doing basic window work is partially embedded: email to Luis says that despite the “Senior Teller / Personal Banker” title he’s “just doing basic window work” and management pushes sales targets, implying he’s not heavily engaged in outbound cross‑selling. 3) Letting newer tellers handle vault/ATM balancing because he’s not comfortable is explicit in the email to Luis (“I almost never touch the vault or ATM balancing – I usually let the newer tellers take that on because… I’m not super comfortable with those reconciliations and I don’t want to be the one who screws them up”).

Sub‑point 3 is clearly embedded: in the text to Danny he says he “basically walked out after our last argument about referrals and didn’t even give notice because I was so over it,” matching quitting without notice after an argument about performance/sales. Sub‑points 1 and 2 (asked to resign after repeated write‑ups; leaving because of compliance rules/monitoring and not wanting to follow procedures) do not appear, but the item as a whole has at least one clearly embedded negative reason for leaving.

1) Handling routine teller transactions and referring anything complicated to manager/lenders due to discomfort is in the 2023‑11‑05 log (“mostly just handle routine transactions and pass anything complicated to the branch manager or the lenders because I’m not comfortable making judgment calls on my own”). 2) Staying late to fix balancing errors after rushing during busy periods appears in the calendar entry (notes about rushing on payday rush and then staying late to fix balancing errors) and the 2022‑01‑28 job reflection (“had to stay almost an hour late just to get back to zero”). 3) Dealing with upset customers after mistakes and tense back‑and‑forths is not explicitly described; there are upset customers and stress, but not that he personally gets into tense back‑and‑forths. Still, the first two sub‑points are clearly embedded.

1) Written up twice for cash‑handling errors and manager suggesting he look for another job before termination is explicitly in the first email to Mami (“written up twice for cash‑handling errors and my manager basically told me I should start looking for another job before it turned into a termination”). 2) Quitting abruptly after disagreement about sales goals with no notice is in the text to Danny (“I basically walked out after our last argument about referrals and didn’t even give notice because I was so over it”). 3) Burnout from long hours and cross‑selling pressure and not wanting to put in extra effort is in the August 18, 2024 email (“worn down by the long hours, the constant push to hit cross‑selling targets, and the feeling that I didn’t have it in me anymore to keep going above and beyond”).

Sub‑point 1 about quarterly audit prep being reassigned due to missed documentation details is not mentioned anywhere. Overall, the negative job‑duty content is well represented.

Also, thanks for being candid in the application about your growth areas. You noted that as a personal banker and senior teller you mostly just follow the scripts the system gives you and pass anything complicated on to the branch manager or loan officers, and that you tend to get flustered when the lobby gets really busy and sometimes fall behind on balancing your drawer until things calm down. That self‑awareness actually helps us when we’re thinking about training plans.

Once I have your confirmation on the GreenLeaf "From Year" for Position 2, I’ll mark your file complete and move you to the next round.

Best regards,

Angela Patel
Talent Acquisition Specialist
Lone Star Community Bank
